title: ANC00093 Blood Clot Formation Around Plaque
text_en:
Formation of a blood clot which can impede blood flow through the artery, leading to a heart attack or a stroke.

---

整段只描述一个医学动作：**血栓形成及其后果**（阻断血流→心梗/中风），属于同一因果链的单一画面展示，因此归为 1 个 logic block。

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A coronary angioplasty procedure is also known as percutaneous coronary intervention. The procedure is done on blood vessels called coronary arteries. They supply your heart muscle with oxygen. The goal is to restore blood flow if a substance called plaque has significantly narrowed these vessels.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: To begin the procedure, the doctor will numb the skin in your wrist. A needle will be placed through your skin and into your radial artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Next, a flexible guide wire will be passed through the needle into your artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Then, the needle will be withdrawn. It will be exchanged for a small, flexible tube called a sheath. This permits access into your artery. You may feel pressure when the doctor inserts the sheath, but you will not feel it moving inside your artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Next, the guide wire will be advanced up to your heart. A flexible tube called a catheter will be advanced over the wire to your coronary arteries. The progress of the procedure will be checked with an x-ray device called a flora scope.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: At this point, your doctor will remove the guide wire. Then, the doctor will move the tip of the catheter just inside the coronary artery to be examined.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A special die will be injected into the artery. This allows your doctor to view it better with a flora scope. The die will make any blockages in the artery stand out.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: If a significant blockage is found, your doctor will insert a guide wire into the artery. A balloon on the tip of the catheter will be moved along the wire to the blockage.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: When the balloon inflates, it will expand the artery and improve the blood flow. You may feel some chest discomfort while this is happening.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: After this, your doctor will deflate and remove the balloon.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A wire mesh tube called a stent may be placed in the treated area. The stent helps keep the coronary artery open. Your doctor will choose the proper size stent, which is compressed over a balloon. The stent will be moved into the artery over the same guide wire.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: When the balloon is inflated, the stent will expand and lock into place. After the balloon catheter is taken out, the stent will stay in place to hold the artery open.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: At the end of the procedure, the guide wire will be removed.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: To find out more about coronary artery angioplasty, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Coronary artery angiography is an x-ray test of the heart. This test can view blood vessels in your heart called coronary arteries. It can find out if a waxy substance called plaque has narrowed these vessels. And, it can show how well the valves and chambers of your heart are working. Coronary angi

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Before the procedure, you will be placed on a table with an x-ray device called a florescope.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Usually cardiac catheterization takes place through the radial artery in your wrist. This route normally has less bleeding and complications. Sometimes the femoral artery in your groin may be used instead.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: To begin, the doctor will numb the skin in your wrist. A needle will be inserted into your radial artery.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: A wire will be passed through this needle. The wire will be gently guided through your arteries to your heart.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: The needle will be taken out and replaced with a small flexible tube called a sheath. This permits access to your radial artery.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Next, a soft flexible tube called a catheter will be slipped over the wire through the sheath. The catheter will be threaded up to your heart.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Then, the florescope will be moved around your chest to take x-ray images of your heart. Your doctor will use the images to check on the catheter. You may feel pressure as the wire and sheath are put in, but you will not feel them inside your arteries.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Once the catheter reaches your coronary arteries, the wire will be removed. The catheter tip will be placed just inside each artery to inject a special die. This die allows the florescope to take x-ray pictures of your arteries, called angiograms.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Any blockages will be clearly identified as the arteries fill with die. You may feel flushed or slightly nauseated when the die is injected.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: At the end of the procedure, the catheter will be removed through your wrist. A pressure band will be used over the radial artery to prevent bleeding. Then, the sheath will be removed. The pressure band will remain on your wrist until bleeding has stopped.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: To find out more about coronary angiography, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: If you or someone you know has recently had a coronary artery bypass graft or cabbage, this video will help you understand the steps of the procedure and how it restores blood flow to your heart.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: A coronary artery bypass graft or cabbage is performed to improve circulation to the heart muscle. In this procedure a healthy artery or vein from another part of the body is connected or grafted to the blocked coronary artery. The grafted artery or vein bypasses the blocked portion of the coronary

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Before the surgery, an intravenous line will be started and you may be given a medication to help you relax. Cabbage procedures are done under general anesthesia, which will put you to sleep for the duration of the operation. A breathing tube will be inserted through your mouth and into your throat

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Coronary artery bypass surgery generally takes 3 to 6 hours. During a conventional bypass surgery, your surgeon begins by making an incision in the skin over your breastbone or sternum. He or she will then cut the sternum and move your ribcage in order to get to your heart.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Throughout the procedure, your circulatory system may be connected to a cardiopulmonary bypass pump or heart lung machine. This machine may temporarily perform the functions of your heart and lungs during the surgery, allowing your heart to be stopped if desired while the surgeon shows the grafts in

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Different blood vessels may typically be used for the grafts. The internal thoracic arteries in the chest, also known as internal mammary arteries, or the saffinous veins in the legs.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: For the internal thoracic artery graft, your surgeon will leave the upper end attached to the subclavian artery and divert the lower end from your chest wall to your coronary artery just beyond the blockage. Your surgeon will then sew the graft into place.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: For the saffinous vein graft, your surgeon will suture one end to the aorta and the other end to the narrowed artery just beyond the blockage.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: With the grafts securely in place, your surgeon may use electrical signals to restore the heart beat and attach a temporary pacemaker to the heart. Once your heart is again beating normally, if the surgeon used the heart lung machine, it would be disconnected.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Your surgeon will wire the breastbone back together and suture the skin incision closed. A temporary drainage tube will be placed through the skin beneath the incision.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: When the surgeon chooses to do the surgery without using the heart lung machine, the heart continues to beat. This may be referred to as off-pump bypass surgery or minimally invasive surgery. Instead, a mechanical device is used to steady the part of the heart where the grafting is being done. Surge

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: After surgery, you will be taken to the intensive care unit. The activity of your heart will be carefully monitored. If necessary, pacing wires will be used to temporarily control your heart rate. The chest tube will remain in place to drain excess blood and air from the chest cavity.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Once you can breathe on your own, your breathing tube will be removed and replaced with an oxygen mask. The bladder catheter will remain in place. As you recover from surgery over the next three to five days, all of these devices will be gradually removed.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Upon discharge from your procedure, be sure to contact your doctor if you have any questions or experience any side effects from the medications you are taking.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Please watch this entire video before caring for your drain. This video will teach you how to take care of your surgical drain.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Curgical drains remove fluid from under your skin near your surgical site. This helps prevent infection and encourages healing.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: A surgical drain consists of a drain tube that runs from your surgical site to a bulb that collects the fluid.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: You strip the drain tube to keep it clear and remove any clots or blockages. Then you empty the bulb when it is half full or as instructed by your healthcare provider.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: You will need alcohol wipes, a measuring cup and a drainage record sheet. Be sure to wear disposable gloves if your healthcare provider says you should.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Be careful not to pull on the tubing. You should not feel any tugging where the tube enters your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 1. Wash your hands with soap and water and then dry them.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 2. Put on disposable gloves if your healthcare provider says you should.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 3. Look for clots or blockages that may prevent the fluid from flowing out of the tube and into the bulb. Some clots may be hidden inside the tube under your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 4. Loosen the clots by gently squeezing the tubes surrounding them.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 5. Use one hand to hold the drain tube in place where it leaves your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 6. Use your other hand to pinch the tube with an alcohol wipe between your finger and thumb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 7. Slide your pinched fingers along the tube to force any fluid out of the tube and into the bulb. You may need to repeat steps 5 through 7 several times to clear the tube. Try not to let go of the tube between steps. If fluid remains in the tube or you accidentally let go, repeat steps 5 throu

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Do not allow the bulb to become more than half full. Too much fluid in the bulb reduces its ability to remove fluid from underneath your skin. Now you will begin emptying the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 8. Hold the bulb lower than your incision so that fluid moves out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 9. Point the bulb away from your body. Never squeeze the bulb before taking the cap off.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 10. Remove the cap. Never touch the opening with your bare hands.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 11. Hold the measuring cup under the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 12. Turn the bulb upside down and squeeze the fluid into the cup.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 13. After removing the fluid, continue squeezing the bulb and use a new alcohol wipe to clean the top.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 14. While still squeezing the bulb, put the cap back on the top. The depressed bulb creates suction that continuously removes fluid from underneath your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 15. Read the amount of fluid in the measuring cup.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 16. Write the amount on your record sheet.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 17. Empty and rinse the cup as directed.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Keep the bulb below the level of your incision to help the fluid move out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice the amount of fluid suddenly increases or decreases, the odor of the fluid changes. The fluid contains pus or becomes thicker over time. Your drain tube falls out or your incision opens. Your incision is red, swollen, painful or has pus coming out or your temperatu

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Please watch this entire video before checking the blood flow in your leg. This video will teach you how to check the blood flow in your leg. Good blood flow is important for recovery after the surgical procedure on your leg.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Look at your foot and toes on the leg recovering from the surgical procedure. Compare your feet. Do you see any differences? Your foot and toes on the surgical side should feel warm, be normal in color, and have little to no swelling. These signs mean you have good blood flow in your leg. If

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Does your foot feel numb or tingly? A feeling of numbness and tingling may also be a sign of poor blood flow. Or, you may feel numbness from a type of anesthesia called a nerve block until it wears off. If you are not sure if you had a nerve block, ask your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 3. Wiggle your toes. Wiggling your toes may improve blood flow to your foot.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps 1 through 3 every 4 hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Another way to check for good blood flow is by doing a capillary refill test. Capillaries are small blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood to your toes. The capillary refill test allows you to see how well blood is flowing through these blood vessels to your toes on the surgical side.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Gently pinch the tip of your toe directly over the toenail with your hand.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Squeeze until your toenail turns white. Then let go. Your toenail should turn pink again within 2-3 seconds. This sign means that you have good blood flow in your leg and foot.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps 1 and 2 every 4 hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice. Your foot looks red or blue. Your foot looks very swollen. Your foot feels cold, numb or tingly, unless you had a nerve block during surgery, making your foot feel numb until it wears off. Or, your toenail takes more than 2-3 seconds to return to a pink color when

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Please watch this entire video before checking the blood flow in your arm. This video will teach you how to check the blood flow in your arm. Good blood flow is important for recovery after the surgical procedure on your arm.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Look at your hand and fingers on the arm recovering from the surgical procedure. Compare your hands. Do you see any differences? Your hand and fingers on the surgical side should feel warm, be normal in color, and have little to no swelling. These signs mean you have good blood flow in your

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Does your hand feel numb and tingly? A feeling of numbness and tingling can also be a sign of poor blood flow. Or, you may feel numbness from a type of anesthesia called a nerve block until it wears off. If you are not sure if you had a nerve block, ask your surgeon.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 3. Wiggle your fingers. Wiggling your fingers may improve blood flow to your hand.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps one through three every four hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

text_en: Your surgeon will perform an abdominal aortic aneurysm repair to prevent the rupture of a bulging area of your aorta.

title: ANH00012 Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
text_en: The largest artery in the body is the aorta, which is attached to the heart and receives blood directly from the left ventricle or main pumping chamber. The aorta is divided into four parts, the ascending aorta, aortic arch, thoracic aorta, and the abdominal aorta.

title: ANH00012 Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
text_en: The abdominal aorta is the last and largest section of the aorta. It supplies blood to the lower regions of your body, including the organs in the abdomen and pelvis. The abdominal aorta ends around naval level, where it splits into the two iliac arteries that carry blood to the legs.

title: ANH00012 Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
text_en: The aorta has a thick wall so it can withstand the high pressure of blood being pumped from the heart. Over time, the wall of the aorta may weaken, slowly bulging outward like a balloon. When this condition occurs in the abdominal region, it is called an abdominal aortic aneurysm.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Atrial fibrillation is a type of arrhythmia, which is an abnormality in the pace or force of your heartbeat. If you have atrial fibrillation, the heart's upper chambers contract in a fast, twitchy fashion, resulting in an irregular, uncoordinated heartbeat.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Your heart is a muscular organ, made up of four chambers, the right and left atria, and the right and left ventricles. During a normal heartbeat, the chambers of your heart contract and relax in a rhythmic pattern to deliver a consistent flow of blood throughout your body.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: The right atrium collects blood from the body, and the right ventricle pumps it to the lungs, which bring oxygen to and remove carbon dioxide from the blood.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: At the same time, the left atrium receives blood from the lungs, and the left ventricle pumps it out to supply your body's tissues with oxygen and nutrients.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Each heartbeat begins in the heart's natural pacemaker called the Sinoatrial or SA node located in the wall of the right atrium. An electrical signal starts here, then spreads across both atria, causing them to contract and push blood into the ventricles.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: As the atria relax, the signal travels through the atrial ventricular or AV node to the ventricular walls, causing them to contract. The ventricles relax, completing one full heartbeat. Immediately the SA node generates a new electrical impulse, repeating the pattern.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: In atrial fibrillation, the electrical signals originate from unusual locations in the atria and nearby pulmonary veins. These abnormal signals travel through the atria haphazardly, causing the atria to contract in a jumble of rapid quivers and twitches.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: The signals bombard the AV node, causing the ventricles to contract rapidly. Even at a faster pace, the ventricular contractions cannot keep up with the rapid atrial contractions, resulting in an uncordenated heartbeat.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: After these irregular contractions, the chambers may not empty completely, when blood collects in the atria, clots may form. A clot can flow out of your heart, through your neck, into your brain, where it can cause

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Your doctor may treat your atrial fibrillation, with anti-arhythmic medications. Your atrial fibrillation control the electrical signals sent to your atria, which help your heart beat in a regular rhythm.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: The innermost layer is called the hypodermis, or subcutaneous layer. This layer stores fat, which cushions the muscles and bones beneath it.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: If you have a pressure injury, your healthcare provider will classify it based on what can be known about the depth of tissue damage. This is called staging.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: In a stage 1 pressure injury, the skin is intact, red, and inflamed. The redness is non-blanchable. This means it doesn't become pale if you press on it.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: A stage 2 pressure injury appears as a blister or an open sore. There is partial thickness loss of the epidermis and dermis. The areas surrounding the sore may be red and irritated.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: A stage 3 pressure injury is a crater-like, full thickness loss of skin. It extends down to the fat tissue of the hypodermis.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: Stage 4 is the most severe. A stage 4 pressure injury is a full thickness loss of tissue. It extends through all three layers of skin and into the underlying muscle or bone.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: An unstageable pressure injury is a full thickness loss of tissue. It's called unstageable because the injury is covered by dead tissue, making it hard to tell how deep it is.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: And a suspected deep tissue injury appears as a maroon or purple area of the skin. It may contain a blister filled with blood. It looks like this because of damaged soft tissue underneath your skin.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: To prevent infection in your pressure injury, your doctor will clean your wound using water and a mild soap or saline solution.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: If your wound is severe, your doctor will debris or surgically remove the dead tissue from the pressure injury.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: To protect your wound and keep it from drying out while it heals, your doctor may cover it with a specialized bandage.

title: ANH12069 Pressure Injuries
text_en: To find out more about pressure injuries, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy
text_en: Your heart is an extraordinary machine. During your lifetime, your heart's muscular walls pump blood into blood vessels, branching throughout your body.

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy
text_en: During a normal heart beat, blood from your tissues and lungs flows into your atria, then into your ventricles.

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy
text_en: Inside your heart, called the inter atrial and interventricular septa, help keep the blood on left and right sides from mixing.

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy
text_en: Strong, thin tissues, called corda tendonae, hold your valves in place during the forceful contractions of your ventricles.

title: ANH12082 Heart Anatomy
text_en: Blood leaving the ventricles passes through another set of valves, the pulmonary valve between your right ventricle and pulmonary trunk, and the aortic valve connecting your left ventricle and aorta.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy

title: ANH14135 Cardiac Arrhythmia
text_en: Depending on the type of aridhmia, a doctor may recommend one or more of the following treatments. Life style changes, such as eating a heart-healthy diet, exercising, and quitting smoking. Medication, such as anti-aridmic drugs and beta blockers, catheter ablation, where thin wires inserted into th

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Depending on activity level, the heart beats about 60 to 100 times per minute. It may be higher during exercise or lower at rest.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: A normal heart rate and rhythm ensures the delivery of oxygen-rich blood to all of the body's organs, such as the brain and lungs.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: A group of cells in the heart, called the cardiac conduction system, uses electrical impulses to control the speed and rhythm of each heartbeat.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: An abnormal heart rate or rhythm, called an arrhythmia, occurs when there's a problem with the heart's conduction system.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Taca-cardia is a type of arrhythmia where the heart beats too fast.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Fibrillation is a type of arrhythmia where the heart beats irregularly and may be too

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: For certain types of arrhythmias, a catheter ablation procedure may be necessary to stop the heart tissue from causing the arrhythmia.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: After numbing a small area in the groin with a needle, the doctor will insert a short hollow tube called a catheter sheath into the femoral vein.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Next, a long flexible tube called a catheter will be inserted through the sheath.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: The doctor will guide the catheter to the heart through a blood vessel that goes to the heart, called the inferior vena-cava. The location and progress of the catheter will be monitored.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: When the catheter reaches the heart, the doctor will guide it to the area that is causing the arrhythmia.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: The doctor will find the problem area is using a 3D map of the electrical activity of the patient's heart.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: The tip of the catheter will emit either hot energy or cold energy to ablate the tissue in this area. The patient makes the treated area stop working.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: For an atrial arrhythmia, a doctor will ablate the atrial tissue causing it. If the affected tissues are small, well-defined areas, the procedure is called focal ablation.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Or if the affected tissues are larger areas with more complex rhythm disturbances, the doctor may perform a procedure called ablation remodeling.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Both types of ablation restore normal electrical impulses and prevent an arrhythmia from happening.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: If the cause of the arrhythmia is in the ventricle, the doctor can do either focal ablation or ablation remodeling to treat more complex arrhythmias of the ventricle.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The heart has four muscular pumping chambers, the right atrium, the left atrium, the right ventricle, and the left ventricle.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The left ventricle pumps blood out through a large artery called the aorta to supply the body with the oxygen and nutrients it needs.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: When left-sided heart failure, the left ventricle is not able to pump enough blood to the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The two types of left-handed heart failure are diastolic, where the left ventricle can't relax enough to fill with blood, and more commonly systolic, where the left ventricle can't contract strongly enough to push blood to the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: In severe or end-stage heart failure, the heart is getting weaker and doesn't respond to medication.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: For left-sided end-stage systolic heart failure, a doctor may recommend an implantable left ventricular assist device, or LVAD, to help the heart pump enough blood to the body. An LVAD is a small mechanical pump implanted inside the chest or abdomen that takes over the job of the weak left ventricle

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: Blood flows from the left ventricle through an inflow tube to the pump. The pump pushes the blood through an outflow tube into the aorta. From here, the blood travels normally from the aorta to the rest of the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: A cable, called a drive line, connects the LVAD pump inside the chest to a control unit located on the outside of the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The control unit is a small computer that controls the functions of the pump and alerts the patient if there are any problems.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The pump and control unit receive power through a cord connected to a battery pack.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: Finally, the control unit and battery pack are held in a lightweight patient pack.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: An LVAD may be a temporary treatment until a heart transplant can be performed, or if the patient isn't able to have a heart transplant, an LVAD may be used for destination therapy, which is long-term treatment.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: You or someone you know may have been diagnosed with high triglycerides. This video will show you what they are and how their levels are measured. High triglycerides can have harmful effects on your body.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: Triglycerides are a type of fat or lipid found in your blood. Your body uses triglycerides for energy.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: One place triglycerides come from is your liver. Another is from the food you eat. Foods that may raise your triglycerides include fatty foods such as hot dogs and tropical oils, refined carbohydrates such as french fries, foods high in simple sugars such as donuts, and alcohol.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: So how do triglycerides get to the cells that need them? Glycerides in food are digested and eventually reach your liver. In the liver, both the triglycerides from your food and those made in your liver are packaged together with proteins. Each resulting molecule is called a lipoprotein.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: The lipoproteins travel throughout the body in your bloodstream. They deliver triglycerides to your cells which use them for energy. Or if you have more than you need, fat cells store the triglycerides as body fat.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: If you are 20 years of age or older, the National Institutes of Health recommends you have a blood test every 5 years. The test is called a fasting lipoprotein profile. During this test, a blood sample will be taken from your arm after you have not eaten for 9-12 hours.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: This blood test can reveal whether your triglycerides fall into a healthy range. For most people, fasting triglycerides should be less than 150 mg per desoliter. 500 or higher puts you into the very high range. This means there is too much fat in your blood.

text_en: In many cases, lifestyle changes can help maintain healthy triglyceride levels. These changes include eating a diet low in sweets, refined carbohydrates and alcohol, eating regular exercise, managing your weight and not smoking.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: Medications can also help treat impaired triglyceride levels. If your doctor prescribed medication to treat high triglycerides, it is important to take it as directed and report any side effects you may have.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: If you or someone you know has been diagnosed with high cholesterol, it's important to keep it under control. This video demonstrates a few ways to help you manage your cholesterol, such as diet, exercise, and medication.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: If your cholesterol levels are too high, it's important to eat a heart-healthy diet. To eat a heart-healthy diet, you need to understand how the types of fats you eat can affect your cholesterol level.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: For example, saturated fats, hydrogenated fats, and trans fats tend to raise the bad cholesterol, also called low-density lipoprotein or LDL cholesterol in your blood. These fats are found in meat and dairy products, tropical oils, and many processed foods, such as cakes and cookies.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Facing saturated fats with unsaturated fats may help lower the bad cholesterol in your blood when eaten as part of a heart-healthy diet. Unsaturated fats include polyunsaturated and mono-unsaturated fats and are found in foods such as salmon, avocados, nuts, and olive oils.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Other heart-healthy lifestyle decisions you can make are to stop smoking and get adequate, moderate intensity aerobic exercise. Walking, biking, or swimming 30 minutes a day, five days a week, may be a good exercise

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Talk to your doctor about the type of diet and exercise plans most appropriate for your health and cholesterol levels.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: If lifestyle changes can't reduce your cholesterol levels enough, your doctor may prescribe certain medications to reduce it.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Modest medicines are the most common medication for lowering LDL or bad cholesterol by reducing the amount made in your liver. However, they have modest effects on lowering another type of fat in your blood called triglycerides, and raising good cholesterol, also called high-density lipoprotein or H

This causes the liver to use more cholesterol to make more bile, thereby decreasing the amount of cholesterol in your bl

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Cholesterol absorption inhibitors block some of the cholesterol taken up by your intestines from the food you eat.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol

In heart disease, blood vessels called coronary arteries become narrowed or blocked by a waxy substance containing cholesterol, called plaque. For time, plaque may reduce or block the flow of oxygen-rich blood to yo

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: There are many known risk factors for heart disease. Risk factors you can control include high blood cholesterol and triglycerides, high blood pressure, diabetes, pre-diabetes, being overweight or obese. Smoking, lack of physical activity, an unhealthy diet, and stress.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: Risk factors you can't control include age, gender, and having a family history of heart

text_en: A healthcare professional can measure your blood pressure to find out if you have high blood pressure. This video will help you understand what the numbers in your blood pressure mean. Your blood pressure can be measured with a blood pressure cuff called a sphigmomanometer.

file: ANH16170_How High Blood Pressure Is Diagnosed.mp4
title: ANH16170 How High Blood Pressure Is Diagnosed
text_en: When your heart beats, the pressure of blood on the walls of your arteries is called systolic pressure.

file: ANH16170_How High Blood Pressure Is Diagnosed.mp4

file: ANH16170_How High Blood Pressure Is Diagnosed.mp4

text_en: If your systolic pressure frequently stays above 130 or your diastolic pressure frequently stays above 80, you have high blood pressure.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: This video will help you understand how blood pressure is controlled in your body. Blood pressure is the amount of force caused by blood pressing against the walls of your arteries. It is tightly controlled to ensure sufficient blood reaches all parts of your body.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: Arteries are blood vessels that carry blood away from your heart to your tissues. This blood supplies your tissues with the oxygen and nutrients they need to function.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: In your heart there are two lower chambers called ventricles which contract with each heart beat. The right ventricle pushes oxygen poor blood to your lungs to pick up oxygen and the left ventricle pushes oxygen rich blood through your arteries to your body.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: As blood flows through your arteries four main factors affect the pressure on the artery walls.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: The first is the amount of blood your ventricles push out of your heart each minute, also known as cardiac output. Your blood pressure goes up as cardiac output increases.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: The second factor affecting your blood pressure is the total amount of blood in your body or blood volume. Blood pressure also goes up as blood volume increases.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: The third factor that affects your blood pressure is resistance. Resistance is anything working against the blood flow through your arteries. Resistance is mainly affected by the width of your arteries. Your body is able to increase the width of your arteries to lower your blood pressure. Or it can

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: The fourth factor that affects your blood pressure is viscosity. Viscosity is how thick your blood is. In your blood more particles such as proteins and fat increase viscosity. If your blood is thicker your blood pressure goes up as your heart works harder to push it through your arteries.

title: ANH16171 Understanding Basic Blood Pressure Control
text_en: If you have questions about high blood pressure or any medications you have been prescribed speak with your doctor. It is important to take your medications as directed and report any side effects you have.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: If you or someone you know has high blood pressure, this video will help you understand what it is and why it's important to keep it under control.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: Over time, high blood pressure will damage the walls of your arteries. This damage can lead to life-threatening conditions.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: For example, an artery wall may become weak and form an enlarged area called an aneurysm. The wall may burst and bleed into the tissue around it.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: In another example, damage to an artery wall may attract certain substances in your blood, such as cholesterol, fat and calcium. They may form a buildup called a plaque. As the plaque gets bigger, blood flow in the artery is reduced.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: Blood cells can stick to the plaque and form solid clumps called clots. Blood clots further reduce or completely block your blood flow.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: If this happens in your brain, it can lead to a stroke.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: If it happens in your heart, it can lead to a heart attack.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: In another example, damage to the arteries in your kidneys may reduce their ability to work properly. This can lead to kidney disease.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: In a final example, damage to your arteries makes your heart beat even harder, which raises your blood pressure even more and can lead to heart failure.

title: ANH16172 Complications of High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text_en: If you have questions about high blood pressure or any medications you have been prescribed for it, speak with your doctor. It is important to take your medications as directed and report any side effects you have.

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: If you or someone you know has high blood pressure, this video will show you some ways to manage it. In most cases, the cause of high blood pressure or hypertension is unknown. This type of high blood pressure is called primary or essential hypertension. Treatment for it includes lifestyle changes s

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: If you are sensitive to the sodium insult, your doctor may tell you to eat less salt and processed foods. Sodium may cause your body to retain water. This increases the amount of your blood and makes your blood pressure go up.

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: Other lifestyle changes can lower your blood pressure. They include not drinking too much alcohol. Getting regular exercise. Losing weight if you need to. And quitting smoking.

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: Your doctor may also recommend medications that act on your kidneys, blood vessels, or heart to help lower your blood pressure. Diuretics commonly called water pills cause your kidneys to move more salt and water from your blood into your urine. This reduces both the amount of your blood and your bl

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: Beta blockers reduce the workload on your heart in two ways. They slow your heart beat and lower the force of each beat.

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: Several types of drugs lower your blood pressure by relaxing your blood vessels. This makes them open up wider. These drugs include ACE inhibitors, angiotensin-2 receptor blockers, calcium channel blockers, and direct acting vasodilators.

title: ANH16173 Treatment for High Blood Pressure
text_en: It is important to stay on your medications as directed even if you are feeling better. Do not go off your medication unless your doctor tells you to. If you have any questions about any medications you have been prescribed, speak with your doctor.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: If you have recently been treated for a heart attack, a condition caused by a blockage of blood flow to your heart muscle, this video will help you understand the condition and its treatment.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Your heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood containing the oxygen and nutrients your body needs. The main pumping chamber of your heart is the left ventricle. In your left ventricle contract, it sends oxygen-rich blood to your body through a large artery called the aorta.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Connected to your aorta are small arteries, called coronary arteries. Blood flows from your aorta through the coronary arteries to supply your heart muscle with oxygen and nutrients.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Using your heart attack, blood flow through one of your coronary arteries may have been severely reduced or completely blocked. Your reduced blood flow may have been caused by a buildup of a fatty substance called plaque in your coronary arteries. If this plaque became disrupted, a blood clot might

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: A blockage in your coronary arteries prevented the oxygen and nutrients in your blood from reaching the part of your heart supplied by the artery. As a result, heart muscle in that area started to die. Damage to part of your heart muscle is called a heart attack. It's also known as a myocardial infa

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Your heart doctor may have recommended a procedure to help open the blockage and improve blood flow to the damaged area. The procedure you had may have been a coronary angioplasty. During a coronary angioplasty, a balloon-tipped catheter inflates inside your blocked coronary artery to open it. The p

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: It's important to know that while the stent opens up an artery in your heart, it will not help with plaque buildup that may be in other arteries in your body.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Or you may have had a coronary artery bypass graft or cabbage. Cabbage is a surgical procedure in which the blocked areas of the coronary arteries are bypassed with veins or other arteries from the body.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Before you left the hospital, your healthcare provider most likely prescribed several medications. Your medication may include the following.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Oral antiplatelet therapy helps prevent platelets from sticking together and forming new blood If you have a stent, this medication helps prevent clots from forming inside it.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: You may have also received drugs called beta blockers that help lower your heart rate and blood pressure.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: Drugs such as angiotensin converting enzyme or ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers or ARBs, and calcium channel blockers also work to lower your blood pressure if needed.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: And you may have been prescribed statins along with a low fat diet to lower your cholesterol. These drugs work by reducing the amount of cholesterol made in your liver. They also help your liver pull cholesterol out of your blood.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: If statins don't work well or have serious side effects, your healthcare provider may prescribe non-statin medications. They work in different ways to lower your cholesterol.

title: ANH16180 About Your Heart Attack
text_en: It is important to stay on your medications as your physician prescribed, even if you are feeling better. Do not go off your medication unless the healthcare professional that prescribed them tells you to.

title: ANH16181 Heart Failure Overview - Basic
text_en: This video will help you understand what heart failure is. Please watch the entire video to learn about heart failure.

title: ANH16181 Heart Failure Overview - Basic
text_en: Your heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood containing the oxygen and nutrients your body needs. Heart failure means that your heart can't pump enough blood around your body. In some cases, heart failure may be temporary, but in most cases, some of the heart damage is perforated.

title: ANH16181 Heart Failure Overview - Basic
text_en: Heart failure can affect both sides of your heart or just one side. Your heart may not pump enough blood to your lungs to pick up enough oxygen. Your heart also may not pump enough oxygen-rich blood out to the rest of your body. This can cause the body to hold onto water and let fluid build up in yo

text_en: Depending on the cause of your heart failure, your health care provider may recommend a procedure or surgery to help your heart. Your provider can tell you more about the best options for your heart failure.

title: ANH16181 Heart Failure Overview - Basic

title: ANH16182 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
text_en: Magnetic resonance imaging, commonly known as MRI, helps doctors find, watch, and treat medical problems. MRI uses very strong magnets, radio waves, and a computer to make pictures of the inside of the body. MRI pictures are more detailed than those made with other methods.

title: ANH16182 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
text_en: There are many reasons to have MRI. MRI may find bleeding, such as bleeding in the brain. Brain changes as in stroke. Problems with your back, such as a herniated disc. And an injury inside the body, such as a torn anterior cruciate ligament.

title: ANH16182 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
text_en: Before MRI, you will remove all metal objects. And it's not safe to have MRI with some medical implants. You should tell the MRI technologist about any metal, device, or implant you may have in your body, such as a pacemaker.

title: ANH16182 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
text_en: To prepare for MRI, you will lie on a table just outside the scanner. MRI scanners are very noisy, so you will probably wear earplugs or headphones. Different equipment, such as pillows and straps, will help you stay in the correct position.

title: ANH16182 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
text_en: Once you are ready, the table will slide into the scanner. Most often, closed scanners, such as this one, are used. The area inside is very narrow.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: This video will help you understand what a stroke is and the risk for having one.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: Normally, blood vessels called arteries carry blood to the brain. The brain needs a constant flow of blood to get the oxygen and nutrients it needs to work properly.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: During a stroke, also known as a brain attack, blood flow to part of the brain has stopped.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: The most common type of stroke is called ischemic stroke. It can happen in an artery where a fatty substance called plaque has made the artery narrow.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: In one type of ischemic stroke called a thrombotic stroke, the plaque can break apart and form a blood clot that blocks the flow of blood to the brain.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: In another type of ischemic stroke called an embolic stroke, a blood clot forms in another area of the body and travels through the blood stream. If it reaches an artery near or in the brain, it can block the flow of blood.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: When an artery to part of the brain is blocked during a stroke, oxygen and nutrients in the blood can't get to those brain cells. The cells start to die within minutes.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: A less common type of stroke is called a hemorrhagic stroke. It occurs when a weakened blood vessel breaks open and leaks blood into the brain. This blocks blood flow to the brain and the leaking blood puts pressure on the brain tissue, which damages it.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: In a transient ischemic attack or TIA, a clot blocks the flow of blood to the brain. But it's different from the other types of stroke because the clot breaks up after a short time. Transient ischemic attacks usually don't cause any permanent damage.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: High blood pressure is the main risk factor for any type of stroke. Other risk factors include diabetes, certain types of heart disease, smoking, high cholesterol, and having previous transient ischemic attacks.

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: It's important to know these signs and symptoms of a stroke, a sudden numbness or weakness in your face, arms and legs, especially if it's on one side of your body. Sudden confusion and trouble speaking or being unable to understand others speaking. Sudden trouble seeing out of one or both eyes. Sud

title: ANH18209 Your Risk of Stroke
text_en: Call 911 right away if you think you or someone else may be having a stroke. To find out more about your risk for having a stroke, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANH19224 What is High Potassium (Hyperkalemia)

title: ANH19224 What is High Potassium (Hyperkalemia)
text_en: The body needs to keep the right amount of potassium in the blood for nerves and muscles including the heart to work normally. title: ANH19224 What is High Potassium (Hyperkalemia)

title: ANH19227 Understanding Leukemia
text_en: Each type of leukemia can be either acute which means it progresses quickly or chronic which means it develops over time.

title: ANH19227 Understanding Leukemia
text_en: Symptoms for leukemia vary depending on the type of leukemia and which blood cells are affected. Sometimes leukemia has no symptoms but in general, the symptoms may include feeling tired and weak, bruising and bleeding easily, fever, weight loss and frequent infections.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: You or someone you care about may have been diagnosed with a blood cancer. This video will help you understand how it can be managed. Blood cancer, also known as hematologic cancer, begins in blood-forming tissue such as bone marrow. Bone marrow is the spongy tissue inside your bones that makes bloo

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Treatments such as chemotherapy, radiation therapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy, and stem cell transplant may control blood cancers depending on the type of cancer and other factors.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: It is important to have a care plan in place not only for receiving therapy and personal care, but also for follow-up care. This includes scheduling and going to follow-up appointments.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: In addition to checking your general health and well-being, your doctor will want to check for any signs of the cancer returning and check for health problems that may have resulted from treatment. For example, you may have tests and imaging studies performed, including taking samples of your blood,

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Continue taking all your medications as prescribed. Do not stop taking them without talking to your doctor.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: It is helpful to take notes for points to discuss that your next doctor's visit. This could include any side effects you may have noticed. If you've had treatments such as chemotherapy, radiation therapy, or a bone marrow transplant, follow any instructions you were given.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: There are lifestyle changes that can improve your health and help you feel better. There are benefits to stopping smoking. This lowers the risk for cancer returning or spreading.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: It's generally accepted that eating a healthy diet can help you feel better and stronger, both during and after treatment.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Physical activities like walking, riding a bicycle or swimming can help you feel better and less tired. Talk to your doctor before starting an exercise routine.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Knowing you have cancer can be overwhelming. You may have worries about your condition and how sick you could get, how it will affect you and your family, treatments and hospital stays, medical bills, and your job.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers

title: ANS00253 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedure

title: Common Causes of Heart Failure
text_en: Any condition or disease that damages your heart's ability to pump blood can cause heart failure.

title: Common Causes of Heart Failure
text_en: For example, the most common cause is coronary artery disease in which blood vessels that supply your heart muscle, called coronary arteries, become blocked by a fatty substance called

title: Common Causes of Heart Failure
text_en: Other common causes of heart failure include uncontrolled high blood pressure and high blood sugar in diabetes.

title: Common Causes of Heart Failure
text_en: Less common causes of heart failure include diseased, infected or damaged heart valves, your regular heartbeats, called arrhythmias, heart defects, poisons or substance abuse, lung diseases, and breathing problems during sleep, called sleep apnea.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: If you have heart failure, your heart has lost the ability to pump enough blood to meet your body's needs.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: You may have weak or damaged ventricular walls that are not able to push enough blood out of your heart.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: You may also have stiff and thickened ventricular walls that do not allow your heart to fill with enough blood.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: If you have left-sided heart failure, your left ventricle does not deliver enough oxygen rich blood to your body, making you feel tired and out of breath.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: Your failing left ventricle also increases the blood pressure in the blood vessels between your lungs and left ventricle. This increased pressure forces fluid out of your blood and into your lung tissues, which makes it difficult for you to breathe.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: If you have right-sided heart failure, your right ventricle is unable to contract with enough force to push blood to your lungs. The result is a build-up of blood in your veins, which causes a build-up of fluid called edema throughout the tissues in your body.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: Over time, heart failure on either side of your heart results in weakened and large ventricles that deliver less blood to your body.

title: Effects of Congestive Heart Failure
text_en: To make up for the decreased amount of blood, your nervous system releases stress hormones that increase the speed and force of your heart beat. Unfortunately, the continued release of these stress hormones makes your heart fail your worse because they damage the muscle cells in your ventricles.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: This video will help you understand some lifestyle changes you can make to treat heart failure. Please watch the entire video to learn about lifestyle changes for heart failure.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: As a heart failure patient, you may need to make some lifestyle changes to prevent further damage to your heart. If you smoke, quitting is one of the most important things you can do for your heart.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Work with your healthcare provider to create a nutritious eating plan that also helps you maintain a healthy weight. A heart healthy diet includes vegetables, fruits, lean meats and whole grains, as well as low fat or fat-free dairy products.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: High calorie foods such as cookies and soda should be replaced with fruits and vegetables that don't contain added salt and sugar.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Examples of foods in a healthy diet include vegetables such as carrots, broccoli and tomatoes, fruits such as apples, oranges and strawberries, lean proteins such as fish, chicken and beans, dairy foods such as low fat yogurt and cheese, starches such as whole wheat bread and sweet potatoes, and hea

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Reduce the amount of salt and sugar you eat. Keep sodium levels below 2000mg to reduce the amount of water retained by your body.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Substitute solid fats with heart healthy oils such as olive oil.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Limit the amount of fluid you drink. Ask your healthcare provider how much fluid you should drink each day. And remember, a heart healthy diet doesn't include alcohol.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Also, check with your healthcare provider about an exercise plan that's right for you. 30 minutes of daily low impact exercise can help strengthen your heart.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Lifestyle Changes
text_en: Talk to your healthcare provider if you have any questions about lifestyle changes to help you manage heart failure.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Medication
text_en: Your doctor may prescribe one or more types of medication to treat your heart failure.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Medication
text_en: Diuretic medications reduce the swelling in your body by increasing the amount of urine produced by your kidneys.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Medication
text_en: Ace inhibitors are medications that allow your blood vessels to expand. This helps decrease your blood pressure and prevents further damage to your heart by making it easier for your heart to pump blood.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Medication
text_en: Beta blockers are medications that block the effects of stress hormones on your heart. Although beta blockers slow down your heartbeat, they are mainly used to protect your heart muscle from the long-term damage caused by stress hormones.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Procedures
text_en: This video will help you understand the main surgical procedures done to treat heart failure. Please watch the entire video to learn about the surgeries for heart failure.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Procedures
text_en: Surgery for advanced heart failure include coronary artery bypass surgery to improve blood flow to your heart muscle.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Procedures
text_en: And heart valve reconstruction surgery to improve blood flow through your heart.

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Procedures
text_en: If the heart remains weak with standard heart failure treatments, advanced treatment options for severe heart failure include insertion of a pump for your heart. A ventricular assist device, or VAD, is a mechanical pump that's used to support heart function and blood flow in people who have weakened

title: Heart Failure Treatment  Procedures
text_en: Another advanced treatment for severe heart failure is a heart transplant.
